prevalence.value: 18-25% annual symptomatic prevalence (including common cold, pharyngitis, and sinusitis)
prevalence.source: DataSUS/VIGITEL; Ministério da Saúde Brazil (2023 report on respiratory infections)
prevalence.trend: Stable with seasonal peaks in winter; slight increase in urban areas due to air pollution

incidence.population: General population, higher in children (up to 6 episodes/year)
incidence.source: SINAN/DataSUS; WHO Global Burden of Disease Study (GBD 2019 adapted for Brazil)

burden.dalyPerCapita: 0.05-0.1 DALYs per capita (primarily from morbidity in acute cases)
burden.mortalityRate: <0.5 deaths per 100,000 population (low, mainly complications in vulnerable groups)
burden.source: IHME Global Burden of Disease (GBD) Brazil estimates 2021; DataSUS mortality data

riskFactors: Air pollution and poor indoor ventilation
Overcrowding in urban and low-income areas
vulnerablePopulations: Indigenous and rural populations with limited access
epidemiology.diagnosticCriteria: Clinical diagnosis based on symptoms such as nasal congestion, sore throat, cough, fever (common cold); pharyngeal erythema and exudate (pharyngitis); facial pain and purulent discharge (sinusitis). Confirmed by rapid antigen tests or culture for bacterial cases (e.g., Streptococcus). Guidelines from Ministério da Saúde.

publicHealthSystem.screeningCoverage: No routine screening; 70-80% access to primary care for symptomatic evaluation (SUS coverage)
publicHealthSystem.controlRate: 60-70% effective control through symptomatic treatment and vaccination (influenza/ pneumococcal)

regionalSpecificities.diagnosisCriteria: Primarily clinical in SUS units; imaging (e.g., sinus X-ray) for complicated sinusitis; adapted from international guidelines with emphasis on viral etiology to reduce antibiotic overuse
regionalSpecificities.treatmentProtocol: Symptomatic relief (analgesics, decongestants); antibiotics only for confirmed bacterial infection (e.g., amoxicillin for streptococcal pharyngitis). Follows Ministério da Saúde protocols, including flu vaccination campaigns
regionalSpecificities.regionalChallenges: High burden in Northern/Northeastern regions due to humidity and poverty; antibiotic resistance from overuse; limited access in Amazonian and rural areas
regionalSpecificities.strongPoints: Robust SUS network for free treatment; national surveillance via SINAN; successful influenza vaccination programs reducing secondary complications
